text: Bicycloalkanes like cubanes and their relatives have recently emerged as important nonclassical bioisosteres for (hetero)arenes in potential drugs. There has been a flurry of new methods developed to meet demand. This review reflects on developments in light as a means to synthesize and modify these valuable species and organics. Reflecting recent advances along different structural trajectories toward functional [1.1.1]-bicyclopentanes (BCPs), [2.1.1]-bicyclohexanes (BCHs), [3.1.1]-bicycloheptanes (BCHeps), cubanes and related bridged species, this article concludes by reflecting on lessons learned and future paths to pursue new light-controlled synthetic pathways to provide future bioisosteres as a means to enhance drugs efficacy in the laboratory.
